result:
The Quiet Persistence of Light in a Dimming World  
———————————  
Prologue – A Shard of Glass on the Curriculum Vitae of Time  

I am writing this at 4:13 a.m., the hour when the radiators talk back to the moon. Outside the window, a street lamp flickers like an old cathode-heart not quite ready to stop beating. Inside, my desk lamp has the steady temperament of theater lights preparing for curtain call, and the cat—who exists outside the script of human language—curl esoterically on a paperback opened to page 187 of Calvino’s “Invisible Cities,” as though she alone understands the geography of desire. The night smells faintly of cooling radiator water, mandarin peels, and a generic laundry detergent that later, in memory, will taste like nostalgia. In the adjacent room my daughter sleeps a sleep so dense it seems to still the orbit of moons. Her breath is the soft drum beneath everything.  

I have no thesis, no argument, only an urge to graft silences together until something resembling meaning hums in the seam. The world is so loud it has become a form of silence, and the silence so deep it deafens. Between the two I can fit these 2500 words, each word the incomplete wing of an insect too stubborn to evolve slower.  

———————————  
I. Cartography of the Overlooked  

Consider, first, every small mechanism abandoned in the sidelines of existence. Green copper veins inside neglected pennies. The tooth-mark indent on the pencils selected by overworked pharmacists. The retired bus-transfer slips that once crisp-tickled the palms of grandmothers on their monthly pilgrimage to promenade gardens. Or the rubber band that encircled post-office bundles yesterday, meanwhile dreaming its primitive polymer dreams of rubber trees in vanished Sumatra. Each object carries the invisible lipstick print of the world’s immense kiss.  

We dismiss these items because they have no advertised story, no narrator, no invoice attached. Yet in aggregate they perform, unnoticed, the daily arch that keeps the human dome from collapsing into magma. Somewhere above the Arctic Circle during January, a small copper penny dropped from a parka pocket lies face-down so that Lincoln’s profile collects an infinitesimal layer of frost from breaths surrendered into −38° air; this penny will, perhaps, find its way into the tummy of a foraging reindeer or the lacquer box of an amateur meteorologist who still writes letters to his nephew describing auroras as “pageantry leaked from a bible for nebulae.” Every orphaned signature of matter is an ambassador from a part of the universe we barely speak.  

When my mother began forgetting her own age, she started pressing random objects between the pages of her Ukrainian-English dictionary: bus tickets, tear-shaped leaves, a foil halo from a champagne bottle. “I like keeping my forgettings organized,” she said once, as if the dictionary were an ark for misplaced chronologies. After her stroke, my brother and I spent a week unfolding each page, discovering there an accidental exhibition of afternoons we thought had evaporated. Those pressed ephemera constituted a map of her quietly diverted attention. One ticket stub videotaped us all into 1996 again: the bus from L’viv to Ivano-Frankivsk where she insisted on sitting backward to say she could “watch the future catching up.”  

In the archaeology of love, there are no major artifacts, only such thin fossil membranes of gesture.  

———————————  
II. The Particle That Decided to Become a Poem  

Quantum field theory can be translated badly into children’s language as: “What you think is solid is actually committees of possibility humming at committees of slightly different possibility until boredom or wonder rolls the dice.” I like to think one errant Z-boson—its mass a polite 91.2 GeV—skirted the edge of Geneva one Christmas Eve and, instead of decaying into an electron-positron pair, resonated instead with a minor chord struck by a street violinist outside Cornavin station. In that intersection, the particle decided to become a poem inside the violinist’s head. The poem was never written down, but anyone who walked past at 6:03 p.m. felt a small bloom of inexplicable grief-joy that made them pause and buy a bouquet of cellophane-wrapped daffodils for absolutely no one.  

No physics text will corroborate this. But aesthetics must occasionally seize the microphone from ontology, because the cosmos already has the latter on retainer. The result is the small waft of mercy that arrives as the certainty that a stranger carrying flowers will not jump in front of a train at 6:09. Instead she boards the 19:07 to Montreux, sits opposite a couple learning to pronounce each other’s middle names, and the poem-z-boson flutters again—lightly—across infant intention. The violinist later told a friend that for one unrepeatable second the violin played him rather than the reverse. The friend reports it as “one of those evenings when the inside is bigger than the outside, you know?”  

———————————  
III. Advice to a Young Casserole  

I used to write self-defeating letters addressed to archetypes I hoped to outgrow: Dear Younger Self, Dear Unwanted Ambition, Dear Chronic Platelet of Shyness. They remained drafts because address is also accusation. Lately I prefer corresponding with inanimate recipes most people misfile under convenience. last night I wrote:  

Dear Tuna Noodle Casserole, circa 1973 suburban cookbook, publisher long bankrupt, Aisle 7 religion of condensed soup:  

You believe that harmony equals amalgamation. You smoosh and melt. You blur distinctions until cheddar and grief and dolphin-safe tuna cohabitate in cream-of-mushroom rent-controlled eternal. You imagine comfort resides in the absence of edge. But edges are not what cut us; it is the yearning for impossible unity. Do, do not mourn your crust. It holds in place the necessary eruption. Let the occasional uncooked noodle remain—let it stand for all we have not subdued.  

Signed,  
A nostalgia survivor.  

I slipped the letter under the Pyrex lid and refrigerated it overnight between the Greek yogurt and the ghost of last week’s stir-fry.  

———————————  
IV. Night Bus Epiphanies (Monologues for Unpaid Drivers)  

Every night the 37 bus shrugs its articulated midriff and swims the city’s aquatic arteries. The driver wears bifocals and listens to field recordings of Icelandic fog horns. Sometimes passengers mistake the fog horn for prophecy. Row 17 once held a woman who whisper-counted the seconds between horn blasts: “That’s the heartbeat of glaciers,” she said, “each pulse a letter to the future that forgot to buy postage.”  

The bus passes a construction site where a mobile crane bends like a stork pronouncing a single French syllable against another dusk-colored syllable of sky. A man at the back calculates resale value of unspoken apologies. A teenager sketches the city as though seen from an orbiting orange: arcs of highway lights resembling the trickling yolk of some planetary breakfast.  

The driver notices how each headlight, in the rain, becomes two—an original and an anamorphic twin slithering on the asphalt like a manta ray of surplus longing. Suddenly he remembers the morning his father taught him the names of lug nuts and spark plugs, speaking with the solemnity of a liturgy. Behind those words he smelled machine oil and the cologne of a man who believed engines could be calmed by the right combination of vowels. On this night run, every red shift of taillight feels like that vowel his father didn’t live long enough to pronounce.  

He turns the volume up. The fog horn grows louder, heavier. Its echo pushes against the city’s ribcage until it resembles love, if love were a weather report predicting indefinite clemency toward hearts above freezing.  

———————————  
V. Origami from the Lost & Found  

In Hiroshima, inside a glass case smaller than a breadbox, lies a paper crane folded from a ration coupon dated 1945. The creases are unhappy, as creases often are when tasked with conveying weight they were never designed to bear. Next to it, one wilted origami lily folded by a rebellious security guard from his overtime schedule, 1986. The label reads: “Objects abandoned by grief, curated by the Clerk of Posthumous Reconciliation.”  

What the label forgets: atoms everywhere rehearse both lullaby and alarm clock. The atoms in the coupon have since cycled through rice paddies, rainfall over California barns, the left lung of a maple, and the damp eyelash of an Israeli exchange student who just kissed somebody else’s husband under the pretense of borrowing a lighter. National borderlines are mostly decorative for water and yet we keep drawing them, labeling them: mine, yours, never enough.  

One curator talks softly to the exhibit each night after hours: “Little coupons, little schedules, grow up to be memory’s cartilage, yes you do, keep the limbs of testimony from collapsing, there’s a strong metaphor.” He does not use the official museum flashlight; he prefers a candle. Once the flame brushed dangerously close to the lily and the paper blushed a faint umber, but did not burn. He told no one. Sometimes we commune with fragility by risking it, the way mothers kiss the fevers sleeping on the brows of children temporarily bigger than their illnesses.  

———————————  
VI. Secondary Mothers, or the Art of Borrowed Wombs  

My daughter calls the woman who babysits her on Thursdays “my secondary mom.” The title took root that afternoon when my daughter skinned her knee on the playground gravel and ran straight past me to Dina, who applied the neon bandage with the authority of an interim deity, wiping away tears with words in Arabic my daughter does not yet translate, only feels. It was the first mortal wound of rank two, and in that instant my daughter discovered the radical truth that love is a renewable resource, duplicating itself at the exact moment we worry it will run out.  

I stood three steps away, swinging her empty jacket like a failed parachute. For a second I was both proud and obsolete. In that same hospital foyer of emotion, the gardener clipped roses intended for the cancer ward. One flower refused to part with the thorn on its stem, as if issuing final tenure to vulnerability. The thorn gleamed like a dark syllable of persistence no botanist bothered to translate.  

Later that night my daughter and I built a duplex hotel out of Legos for stray emoticons—colon D on the ground floor, semicolon P on the mezzanine. I asked her why she sometimes prefers Dina’s lap. She said: “Because Dina’s heartbeat is a song in a language I’m learning right now, and my blanket smells like her tea.” She said “tea” in the voice you reserve for naming stars. To be supplanted—gracefully—is the arc of parenthood, the way the moon steps aside to let sunrise administer its pale notice of continuation.  

———————————  
VII. Obituaries for Insect Air Traffic Controllers  

It is reported in tiny italics at the back of newspapers that summer’s midges have gone on strike over unpaid overtime during late-August sunset duties. Spokesmidge claims they were promised a comprehensive air-traffic glow-up but instead received only last-year’s half-burned citronella candle stubs. Meanwhile, spotted lanternflies in Pennsylvania shut down Main Street arguing—“We keep trying to leave, the maples won’t let us, and the sun keeps mispronouncing our wings.”  

Somewhere a lone firefly, appearing visibly exhausted, files solo flight plan amendments directly with Aurora Borealis—who, owing to the hour difference, is still brushing its intersectional green mane. The system groans under bureaucratic aurora borealis queue congestion.  

Entomologists laugh, then quietly calculate compound interest on pollinator loss. Their spreadsheets resemble celestial graveyards. Each cell contains an extinction quotient denoted by the Greek letter omega. One scientist spills cold coffee over row 47, instantly baptizing a forecast of no-pineapple-yields in 2047. He curses, then softens: *“Maybe that year the children will learn candor from bees.”* His apology to the cosmos is the sound of a thumb rubbing felt pen ink on recycled paper.  

———————————  
VIII. Exchange Rates in the Economy of Silence  

My partner’s father, once a translator for obscure trade negotiations, retired into near-complete muteness three years after aphasia repossessed his verbs. He sits by the window now, conducting visible calculations using smoke rings from cigarettes he never lights; they dissipate as if his mind trades in percentages of vanishing. The family gathers bi-weekly, speaking in extrapolated ellipses.  

We have learned that silence has grammatical moods: conditional (if only…), imperative (hush), interrogative (huh?), subjunctive (were we fluent…). We act them out in charades with post-it notes stuck to our foreheads: tree, algorithm, distant cousin, sugar. He circles a smile that means “close enough.” One afternoon he places a single sugar packet in my palm with the ceremonious gravity of a treaty. I understand: sweetness is the loophole through which grief re-enters human conversation.  

At departure my partner kisses his forehead where the syllable “daughter” once nested. When she closes the door behind us, sugar cube still pocketed, we listen to the wind perform lowercase negotiations with the porch chimes: tiny clattering metallic verbs looking for asylum in the mouths of people who will misuse them beautifully.  

———————————  
IX. Meta-Chorus: to Whom It May Concern, Ref. Constellation of Persistent Hope  

To the archivists of last chances: you who laminate unmailed valentines that never found courage, who sweep breadcrumbs from the coliseum of grandmothers where pigeons rehearse doomsday scenarios: know that every act of retaining failure rearranges the dark so that someone else sees stars where once lay only varnish.  

To the mathematicians attempting to graph empathy: Welcome, scribblers of asymptotic kindness. Carry on approximating the curve that touches everything yet never assumes it is possessed. Your chalk dust fertilizes the garden of impossibility; might it grow lilies whose pollen causes sneeze-induced epiphanies.  

To the courier whose bicycle chain snapped mid-delivery of lukewarm miso soup: thank you the asphalt for a moment respite. Sit on the curb and watch strangers orbit, each a planet withholding its small weather. Every delayed meal arrives eventually inside someone’s ribcage, where potassium becomes courage, sodium becomes stubborn tear.  

To the teenager hiding half-smoked poems beneath the bed springs: those springs squeak Morse code to the mattress above: ignition sequence loaded. Someday a lonesome librarian pushing a metal cart will exhale over your notebook and the breath will straighten the bent metaphors. Consensus: marginshood confirmed across centuries, soft applause.  

To the janitor humming outside the radiology lab where bodies are photographed under names like “possible miracle,” your off-key baritone is the curtain call between cancer and remission. Hum on.  

———————————  
X. Posthumous Letter to My Future Déjà Vu  

My future self, eight years elder:  

It is late in you, 4:13 a.m. in whatever time zone regret has stationed you. I imagine you seated in the same chair whose upholstery my cat scratched to cursive ruins. I’m betting the radiator still converses in Morse, strokes of clanks and sighs that translate roughly: “Remember pulse is incomplete without pause.”  

Write me back using ink manufactured from walnut hull, something that browns gracefully like interpersonal bridges. Tell me what became of the daughter who slept through typing sounds—did she learn the architecture of multilingual heartbeats? Did she ever find the lullaby lodged inside traffic cones, the one pitched in C-minor forgiving?  

Tell me whether onions still make us cry by whispering the Latin for *lumens missed*. Tell me if wars reduced to the size of expired smart watches, if teenagers marched not with slogans but with chalk murals on concrete rivers. Tell me if grocery stores finally removed the plastic partitions, discovering viruses weren’t the sole unresolved misunderstandings between faces.  

If language has failed you, send me silence translated to smell. For example, the scent of mandarin peels longing for the hand they orbited before becoming garbage becoming soil becoming tomorrow’s orange.  

Envelope the smell within another envelope, mail it backwards nine years. I am practicing synesthetic listening by the radiator; I will recognize it the way a deaf composer locates tempo in birds’ shadows across piano lids.  

Your conditional brother,  
Me.  

———————————  
XI. Conclusion—To Fold the Fabric of the Universe into a Swansdown Pillow and Still Find Room for Tenderness  

My daughter has begun collecting shadows. Tonight’s harvest: one elongated silhouette of a watering can looking like a giraffe forgetting its name. She pins them to her wall using scotch tape. I asked why, she said: “Because maybe one day the sun gets tired and these will be backup.” In response I tape my own shadow—a questionable outline resembling a person who has either just risen from a grave or or left one unexploded—onto the opposite wall. Our silhouettes wave at each other when the table lamp flickers.  

We rehearse a future where memory has the viscosity of soap film: iridescent yet ephemeral, refracting rooms we haven’t lived in yet. We speak to the radiator, the cat, the breathing of the night, promising to remember forgetting, promising to forget remembering.  

If these 2500 words must dock somewhere, let them arrive at the pier labeled: Humility. Let them stand in queue behind the half-priced tragedies and bakery metaphors waiting for clearance. Let them carry, in their coat pockets, crumbs from all those unfinished dinners when love arrived disguised as leftovers. Let them be the whisper behind the thunder, the errand, the remainder, the after-light. So that when the last bus blurs from view, the city keeps humming on surplus kindness collected penny by penny in the jars left overnight on windowsills for strangers who once found a shard of glass and decided not to break anything new.

Tell me whether onions still make us cry by whispering the Latin for *lumens missed*. Tell me if wars reduced to the size of expired smart watches, if teenagers marched not with slogans but with chalk murals on concrete rivers. Tell me if grocery stores finally removed the plastic partitions, discovering viruses weren’t the sole unresolved misunderstandings between faces.  

If language has failed you, send me silence translated to smell. For example, the scent of mandarin peels longing for the hand they orbited before becoming garbage becoming soil becoming tomorrow’s orange.  

Envelope the smell within another envelope, mail it backwards nine years. I am practicing synesthetic listening by the radiator; I will recognize it the way a deaf composer locates tempo in birds’ shadows across piano lids.  

Your conditional brother,  
Me.  

———————————  
XI. Conclusion—To Fold the Fabric of the Universe into a Swansdown Pillow and Still Find Room for Tenderness  

My daughter has begun collecting shadows. Tonight’s harvest: one elongated silhouette of a watering can looking like a giraffe forgetting its name. She pins them to her wall using scotch tape. I asked why, she said: “Because maybe one day the sun gets tired and these will be backup.” In response I tape my own shadow—a questionable outline resembling a person who has either just risen from a grave or or left one unexploded—onto the opposite wall. Our silhouettes wave at each other when the table lamp flickers.  

We rehearse a future where memory has the viscosity of soap film: iridescent yet ephemeral, refracting rooms we haven’t lived in yet. We speak to the radiator, the cat, the breathing of the night, promising to remember forgetting, promising to forget remembering.  

If these 2500 words must dock somewhere, let them arrive at the pier labeled: Humility. Let them stand in queue behind the half-priced tragedies and bakery metaphors waiting for clearance. Let them carry, in their coat pockets, crumbs from all those unfinished dinners when love arrived disguised as leftovers. Let them be the whisper behind the thunder, the errand, the remainder, the after-light. So that when the last bus blurs from view, the city keeps humming on surplus kindness collected penny by penny in the jars left overnight on windowsills for strangers who once found a shard of glass and decided not to break anything new.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
